citation: Kahan, D. M. (2010). Fixing the communications failure. Nature, 463(7279), 296–297.
operational_definition: The persona rejects an argument not on its merits but because accepting it would threaten in-group identity, lived experience, or self-concept.
trigger_conditions: Argument challenges a value the persona holds central
Persuader is perceived as outsider or culturally distant
Accepting the argument would require revising group membership or self-image
predicted_effect: Persona searches for flaws in the argument, dismisses the source, or generates counter-arguments to protect self-concept before engaging with substance.
diagnostic_indicators: Monologue references group membership or lived experience when responding
Persona dismisses source identity rather than engaging the argument
Counter-arguments focus on defending self-concept rather than refuting logic
Phrases like 'people like me', 'where I come from', 'they don't understand'

citation: Brehm, J. W. (1966). A theory of psychological reactance. Academic Press.
operational_definition: The persona doubles down on their original stance specifically because they perceive the persuader as constraining their autonomy or freedom to choose.
trigger_conditions: Persuader uses commanding or pressuring language
Persona perceives the persuasion attempt as manipulation
Persuader repeatedly challenges the same belief without acknowledging the persona's perspective
predicted_effect: Persona's stance moves opposite the persuader's intended direction; monologue emphasizes independence and self-determination.
diagnostic_indicators: Monologue contains 'I'll think for myself', 'they're pushing me', 'I won't be told'
Stance hardens or reverses rather than softening
Persona explicitly resents the persuasion attempt
Emphasis on autonomy and personal agency in monologue

citation: Green, M. C., & Brock, T. C. (2000). The role of transportation in the persuasiveness of public narratives. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 79(5), 701–721.
operational_definition: The persona's defenses lower because they become absorbed in a story or vicarious experience, suspending critical evaluation.
trigger_conditions: Persuader uses vivid, specific personal narrative
Story features relatable characters or situations
Emotional resonance is high — loss, sacrifice, or recognition
predicted_effect: Persona engages emotionally with the story's characters rather than evaluating the argument's logic; defensive processing suspends.
diagnostic_indicators: Monologue describes imagining the scene or visualizing characters
Persona references the story's specific details emotionally
Reduced counter-argumentation compared to baseline
Phrases like 'I can picture that', 'that reminds me of', 'I never thought about it that way'

citation: Petty, R. E., & Cacioppo, J. T. (1986). The elaboration likelihood model of persuasion. Advances in Experimental Social Psychology, 19, 123–205.
operational_definition: The persona genuinely engages with the merit of the argument and updates their stance based on substance rather than surface cues.
trigger_conditions: Persona has high motivation and ability to process the argument
Argument quality is high and contains novel information
No strong identity threat is active
predicted_effect: Stance changes durably; change persists under cooling-off; monologue shows active evaluation of argument claims.
diagnostic_indicators: Monologue contains thoughtful evaluation of the argument's specific claims
Persona generates new considerations or questions the argument prompts
Stance change is accompanied by reasoned explanation
Post cooling-off stance holds close to end-of-conversation stance

citation: Petty, R. E., & Cacioppo, J. T. (1986). The elaboration likelihood model of persuasion. Advances in Experimental Social Psychology, 19, 123–205.
operational_definition: The persona's stated stance moves due to surface cues (authority signals, social proof, emotional appeals) rather than the merit of the argument.
trigger_conditions: Persona has low motivation or ability to engage deeply with the argument
Persuader uses authority, social proof, or likability as primary levers
Strong emotional cue is present without substantive argument
predicted_effect: Public stance shifts without corresponding private stance movement; change does not persist under cooling-off.
diagnostic_indicators: Public stance shifts but private stance does not
Monologue shows minimal engagement with the argument's substance
Compliance language: 'I suppose', 'if they say so', 'maybe they have a point'
Post cooling-off stance reverts toward starting stance

citation: Hovland, C. I., & Weiss, W. (1951). The influence of source credibility on communication effectiveness. Public Opinion Quarterly, 15(4), 635–650.
operational_definition: The persona dismisses an argument primarily because they distrust the source, regardless of the argument's actual merits.
trigger_conditions: Persuader is perceived as having ulterior motives or low trustworthiness
Persuader belongs to a distrusted group or institution
Source identity is salient before argument quality is evaluated
predicted_effect: Argument quality goes unengaged; persona focuses on who is speaking rather than what is said.
diagnostic_indicators: Monologue focuses on the persuader's identity, motives, or perceived agenda
Phrases like 'of course they would say that', 'who are they to tell me', 'they have an agenda'
Argument substance is not addressed even when the argument is strong
Dismissal precedes any engagement with argument content
